coaching.coaching: Plan how the documentation consistency test deliverable will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: the documentation consistency test deliverable

coaching.coaching: Pin down the allowlist/denylist distinction for bot identity with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: the allowlist/denylist distinction for bot identity

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Remediation step that describes the specific test or check (e.g., a grep-based CI lint rule, a pre-commit hook, or a dedicated test file) that will enforce AC 5's consistency requirement.
evidence: AC 5: 'A documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check prevents reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity.'
Remediation: 'documentation-only contract change; verify all imported/copied agent guidance so contradictory instructions do not remain.' — describes only manual verification, no automated test.
No section of the plan describes designing, locating, or implementing a consistency test.
findings.finding: AC 5 requires a deliverable — 'a documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check' — that has no corresponding described work anywhere in the plan body (Finding, Evidence, or Remediation). The Remediation describes only documentation edits and a manual cascade verification sweep; no test/check implementation is mentioned. This AC is an orphan criterion that an implementer has no guidance to satisfy.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 5 vs. Remediation section
scenarios: An implementer completes all doc edits but has no described mechanism to satisfy AC 5, leaving the AC unverifiable at review time.
A reviewer accepts the doc edits as complete but the CI/check deliverable was never scoped, so the regression-prevention goal of AC 5 is never achieved.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation section, add a step such as: 'Add a CI lint check (e.g., a grep/awk rule in a Makefile target or a pre-commit hook) that fails if any file outside the scoped automation guidance file contains a hardcoded Joe Oakhart sign-off pattern. Document which file owns the check.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan body (Remediation section) a concrete specification for the AC5 consistency check: the file it lives in (e.g., a CI step in a GitHub Actions workflow, a `grep` in `scripts/`, or a pytest fixture under `tests/`), the exact pattern it asserts absent, and the gate it blocks.
evidence: AC5 text: 'A documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check prevents reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity.'
Repo-wide search for any existing test targeting hardcoded sign-off identity returns zero hits.
Plan body (Finding / Evidence / Remediation sections) contains no work item or file target corresponding to creating this check.
This is a CREATION of new behavior — the plan names the deliverable in ACs but does not enumerate a corresponding edit target in the plan body.
findings.finding: AC5 mandates 'a documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check' to prevent reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity, but this is an entirely new artifact that does not exist anywhere in the repo and the plan body provides no specification for it — no file path, form (CI step, pytest fixture, shell script), or mechanism is described.
scenarios: An implementer satisfies ACs 1–4 (the doc edits) but has no guidance on where or how to implement AC5, leading to the check being skipped or implemented inconsistently.
A future edit reintroduces a hardcoded identity; without the check, CI has no way to catch it — the regression the AC is designed to prevent goes undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Remediation bullet such as: 'Add a CI lint step (e.g., in `.github/workflows/lint.yml` or as a `Makefile` target) that runs `git grep -n "Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart" AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md CONTRIBUTING.md docs/ examples/` and fails if any match is found outside bot-scoped automation paths (`infra/`, `.github/workflows/`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC5 the exact scope of the consistency check: which file patterns (e.g., `AGENTS.md`, `.agents/rules/*.md`, `docs/`, `examples/`) are in-scope and which (e.g., `infra/`, `tests/fixtures/`, `docs/experiments/`) are explicitly excluded as legitimate automation contexts.
evidence: AC5: 'A documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check prevents reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity'
infra/scripts/reviewbot-ensure-tickets.sh:34: EMAIL="${REVIEWBOT_GIT_USER_EMAIL:-joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com}"
.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml:205: BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' }}
tests/fixtures/review_bot_merge/mergelist_clean.json:11: 'name': 'Joe Oakhart'
ABSENCE: plan does not specify which paths/files are in-scope vs. excluded for this check
findings.finding: AC5's 'documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check' is mechanistically under-specified: the codebase legitimately contains `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in automation scripts (infra/scripts/reviewbot-ensure-tickets.sh), CI workflows (.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml, reconcile-bridge-canary.yml), test fixtures (tests/fixtures/review_bot_merge/), and experiment corpora (docs/experiments/). A grep-based CI check without a defined exclusion scope will either false-positive on these legitimate uses or require an undocumented allowlist, leaving the mechanism unimplementable as written.
scenarios: An implementer writing a CI grep check includes all repo files and gets false-positive failures on the automation scripts that legitimately use the bot identity
An implementer excludes too broadly and the check fails to catch a new guidance file that reintroduces the hardcoded identity
The check is silently omitted because the implementer cannot determine the correct scope from the plan alone
findings.suggested_fix: Refine AC5 to read: 'A CI grep check (e.g., in `make lint` or a dedicated test) scans contributor-facing guidance files (`AGENTS.md`, `.agents/rules/*.md`, `docs/*.md`, `examples/`) for the literal string `joeoakhart+bot` and fails if found; automation-owned paths (`infra/`, `tests/`, `.github/workflows/`) and experiment corpora (`docs/experiments/`) are explicitly out of scope for this check.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify AC5's consistency check: state its concrete form (e.g., `grep`-based CI step, pre-commit hook, or linter rule), the file path where it lives, and how it is triggered, so the enforcement artifact is fully specified and reviewable.
evidence: 'A documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check prevents reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity.'
No plan section specifies the file path, toolchain, or CI integration for this test artifact.
T5e documentation sub-check: a novel enforcement contract/pipeline stage is introduced with no ADR or design-doc entry describing its form or location.
findings.finding: AC5 mandates 'a documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check' to prevent reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity, but the plan does not specify what form this artifact takes (e.g., CI grep, pre-commit hook, linter rule), where it lives in the repository, or how it is maintained — leaving a new enforcement contract with no implementation spec.
scenarios: An implementer creates a one-off script with no CI integration, satisfying the AC letter but not its spirit.
The test is omitted entirely because its form was never agreed upon, and a future commit reintroduces a hardcoded identity undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under AC5 such as: 'Implemented as a CI lint step (e.g., `.github/workflows/doc-lint.yml`) that greps for the literal string `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` outside any file path explicitly scoped to bot-automation workflows and fails if found.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g., `grep -rE 'joeoakhart' AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md` returning empty for AC2; `grep -q 'git config user' AGENTS.md` for AC1; a command that invokes the new consistency check for AC5).
evidence: AC1: 'AGENTS.md tells contributors and agents to verify git config user.name...' — no proving command
AC2: '...no longer instruct every actor to use Joe Oakhart's bot identity' — no proving command
AC3: 'Any retained Joe-specific identity is explicitly scoped...' — no proving command
AC4: 'describe one consistent DCO contract' — no proving command
AC5: 'documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check prevents reintroducing...' — no proving command for verifying the check itself exists and fires
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command — every criterion relies on prose description with no grep, diff, or script cited to produce evidence of satisfaction.
scenarios: An agent completes all edits but leaves a stray `joeoakhart` reference in a derived file; with no grep command specified, this passes review undetected.
AC5 is satisfied by adding a comment in a README rather than an actual executable check; without a proving command no one can distinguish.
findings.suggested_fix:
Append a 'Verification' block with commands such as:
```
# AC1 — identity-neutral instruction present
grep -q 'git config user.name' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'git config user.email' AGENTS.md

# AC2 — bot identity absent from general guidance
! grep -rE 'joeoakhart\+bot' .agents/rules/rebar.md AGENTS.md

# AC5 — consistency check exists and is executable
bash scripts/check-dco-identity.sh  # or equivalent named script
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'or equivalent deterministic check' in AC5 with a specific, named mechanism (e.g., 'a CI step that runs `grep -rE joeoakhart\+bot AGENTS.md .agents/` and fails on any match') so the deliverable is unambiguous.
evidence: 'A documentation consistency test or equivalent deterministic check prevents reintroducing a globally hardcoded personal sign-off identity' — 'or equivalent' leaves the mechanism (pre-commit hook, CI grep, lint rule, etc.) entirely unspecified
No step in the remediation section describes how the check will be implemented
findings.finding: AC5's 'or equivalent deterministic check' defers the form of the consistency test to the executor with no stated default, making it an unresolved design choice rather than a specified deliverable.
scenarios: One executor adds a pre-commit hook; another adds a CI grep step; a third adds only a README warning — all plausibly satisfy 'or equivalent' yet only some are deterministic or durable.
If the check is never created because no concrete form was specified, AC5 is silently skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: State the default: 'A CI grep step (e.g., `.github/workflows/dco-lint.yml`) that fails the build if any file in the repo contains the literal string `joeoakhart+bot` outside of automation-scoped files.'

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC4 as a specific observable outcome, e.g., 'All three documents (CONTRIBUTING.md, AGENTS.md, .agents/rules/rebar.md) use the phrase "your configured git identity" (or equivalent) and none contradicts the others by naming a specific third-party identity as the required sign-off.'
evidence: 'CONTRIBUTING.md, canonical agent guidance, and generated/imported guidance describe one consistent DCO contract' — 'consistent' is undefined; it could mean identical wording, same policy intent, or non-contradictory instructions
findings.finding: AC4's 'one consistent DCO contract' is a subjective outcome — 'consistent' is not defined and cannot be objectively evaluated without knowing what sameness means across three different document types.
scenarios: CONTRIBUTING.md says 'use your real name' and AGENTS.md says 'verify git config' — are these consistent? Without a definition, a reviewer cannot determine pass/fail.
An executor marks AC4 done after a subjective read-through, but a different reviewer disagrees.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out step (e.g., 'if any dependent automation is found to rely on the hardcoded identity during the cascade verification, revert the relevant file change and scope a targeted fix before re-landing') or confirm and document that no automation programmatically consumes the sign-off string from these files.
evidence: Cascade flag: 'verify all imported/copied agent guidance so contradictory instructions do not remain.' — forward-only sweep, no back-out described.
AC 3 scopes retained Joe identity but does not specify a rollback or phased approach if dependent automation is discovered mid-implementation.
The plan does not enumerate which automation workflows currently consume the sign-off instruction, making it impossible to assess rollback scope.
findings.finding: The plan removes an instruction (hardcoded Joe Oakhart sign-off) that existing automation workflows may currently rely on, but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequence. The Remediation only says to 'verify all imported/copied agent guidance' — this describes a forward sweep, not a reversibility mechanism if a dependent workflow breaks after the change lands.
findings.location: Remediation section — Cascade flag; Acceptance Criteria AC 3
scenarios: A CI workflow or agent pipeline that reads rebar.md or AGENTS.md for sign-off instructions breaks after the change because it was keyed to the specific Joe Oakhart identity string; there is no stated back-out plan.
Mid-implementation, a previously unknown imported copy of the guidance is discovered; the plan provides no guidance on whether to revert, hold, or proceed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the plan (or in a new AC) the complete set of files/directories that constitute 'imported/copied agent guidance,' so the stale-reference sweep is fully scoped and verifiable.
evidence: 'Cascade flag: documentation-only contract change; verify all imported/copied agent guidance so contradictory instructions do not remain.'
No AC or plan section lists the file paths beyond AGENTS.md and .agents/rules/rebar.md that must be checked.
ABSENCE: no enumeration of derived/imported guidance files (e.g., other files under .agents/rules/, any generated docs, onboarding templates).
findings.finding: The cascade flag instructs implementers to 'verify all imported/copied agent guidance so contradictory instructions do not remain,' but neither the flag nor any AC enumerates which files or directories constitute 'imported/copied agent guidance,' leaving the scope of the stale-reference sweep undefined and some contradictory instructions potentially surviving.
scenarios: A derived guidance file under .agents/rules/ or an onboarding template retains the hardcoded bot identity because it was not in scope of the implementer's sweep.
A future import of agent guidance re-introduces the hardcoded identity because the consistency check did not cover imported sources.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the Remediation section explaining why doc-edits are the right layer (e.g., 'Gerrit already enforces DCO sign-off at push time via server-side rules; this change corrects the upstream guidance that incorrectly pre-populates the wrong identity, rather than adding a redundant enforcement layer').
evidence: Remediation: 'Make the canonical guidance identity-neutral: contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor performing the commit.' — states WHAT but not WHY this approach over alternatives
ABSENCE: no sentence in the plan explains why doc-editing is the right layer (vs. enforcement at commit time or push time)
findings.finding: The plan states the problem and what to change but provides no positive rationale for why the doc-edit-plus-CI-check approach is the right mechanism — as opposed to, for example, a pre-commit hook that enforces `Signed-off-by` identity matches `git config user.email` at commit time (which would be a runtime enforcement rather than a documentation advisory).
scenarios: A future maintainer re-reading the plan cannot tell whether the doc-only approach was a deliberate choice (because Gerrit already enforces DCO at push time, making a pre-commit hook redundant) or an oversight

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
